ApplicationIdp.description:
A brokered upstream IdP for a federated Application (API response shape).

``id``, ``org_id``, ``created_at``, ``updated_at``, and ``deleted_at`` come
from ``OrgScopedEntity``. The client secret is transparently encrypted via
``EncryptedModelMixin``: in the database it lives only in the
``client_secret_encrypted`` BYTEA column (AES-256-GCM, AAD bound to the row's
identity), which API-facing selects omit entirely. The plaintext
``client_secret`` below is a write-only/decrypt-only carrier — it is ``None``
on every API read and is populated only by the repository's ``get_with_secret``
path (``from_encrypted``), consumed by the provisioning handler.

audience_client_id.description: Per-app Zitadel customers-org OIDC application (SPA) client_id; the brokered id_token's aud/azp. NULL until provisioned. The token verifier ADDITIONALLY requires the presented id_token's audience to match this value, but ONLY when it is non-null (additive hardening — the aud check is skipped while NULL, so behavior is unchanged until provisioning populates it). For a jwks application's direct federations this column is reused as the OPTIONAL expected `aud` of the partner IdP's JWT (same skip-while-NULL semantics; nothing provisions it).
audience_client_id.title: Audience Client Id

client_id.description: OAuth client_id Introspection uses against the brokered customer IdP.
client_id.title: Client Id

client_secret.description: Plaintext brokered customer IdP client secret. Encrypted at rest via EncryptedModelMixin; NEVER returned by API-facing reads — populated only by get_with_secret for provisioning.
client_secret.title: Client Secret

disabled_at.description: IMMEDIATE revoke timestamp. A disabled federation is inactive (frees a slot) and excluded from verifier lookups.
disabled_at.title: Disabled At

external_idp_id.description: The Zitadel IdP id; NULL until provisioned.
external_idp_id.title: External Idp Id

external_org_id.description: Per-federation domainless Zitadel customers org id; NULL until provisioned. The token verifier binds a presented id_token's resourceowner org to this value.
external_org_id.title: External Org Id

idp_provider.description: Vendor label for the brokered customer IdP (okta / supabase / auth0 / neon / generic); a UI hint only.

is_active.description:
A federation occupies a slot (and is verifier-eligible) while neither
soft-deleted nor disabled. A revoke (``disabled_at``) is immediate — there
is no grace window, so no ``expires_at`` to consider (cf. ApplicationSecret).
is_active.title: Is Active

issuer.description: Brokered customer IdP OIDC issuer (e.g. https://<tenant>.okta.com or https://<ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1).
issuer.title: Issuer

scopes.description: OIDC scopes requested from the brokered customer IdP.
scopes.title: Scopes

ApplicationIdp.title: ApplicationIdp

ApplicationIdpProvider.description:
Vendor label for the brokered customer IdP.

A UI hint that drives default issuer URLs / scopes per vendor; the federation
itself stays provider-agnostic and runs purely off issuer / client_id /
scopes. ``generic`` is any other OIDC issuer. This is the VENDOR, distinct
from Zitadel's protocol-level IdP type (which is OIDC for all of these).

``ZITADEL`` is the exception — not an external vendor but Introspection's
hosted built-in login for an spa application's end users: provisioning
enables Zitadel-native username/password registration on the per-federation
customers org instead of registering a custom upstream. No issuer, no
client credentials; the end user signs in (or registers) at the customers
org's hosted login and the brokered token-exchange path is unchanged.

``NEON`` (Neon Auth) is app-level only for now: the Postgres
``application_idp_provider`` enum type (created on main, not this branch)
does not yet include ``'neon'``, so persisting it requires a follow-up
``ALTER TYPE application_idp_provider ADD VALUE IF NOT EXISTS 'neon'``
migration on main. Until then, rows for Neon-backed federations carry the
``generic`` vendor label (the federation is provider-agnostic either way).
ApplicationIdpProvider.title: ApplicationIdpProvider

ApplicationIdpStatus.description:
Provisioning lifecycle of the brokered IdP in Zitadel.

A ``jwks`` application's direct federations have nothing to provision —
they are created ``ACTIVE`` immediately and never pass through
``PENDING`` / ``FAILED``.
ApplicationIdpStatus.title: ApplicationIdpStatus

ApplicationType.description:
The kind of OAuth client.

Drives the primary grant and whether a client secret is issued (confidential:
``service_account``). PKCE is always enforced on any auth-code flow, so it is
not stored. The ``spa`` browser B2B2C type uses Introspection's own hosted
login; it becomes federated-capable by attaching brokered customer IdP(s)
(Zitadel registers each as an upstream IdP, recorded in the ``application_idps``
join). It is a public ``authorization_code`` client; the end user becomes a
``customer`` member. See applications-and-oauth.md §3.1 / §5.0★.

The ``jwks`` type is the §5.0★★ direct-federation client: its users sign in
at the customer's own IdP and the SPA exchanges that IdP's JWT via the
RFC 8693 token-exchange grant. It runs NO OAuth grant of its own — no
redirect URIs, no hosted login, no secret — so its grant list is empty and
its ``application_idps`` rows are always ``direct_jwks``.
ApplicationType.title: ApplicationType

BrokeredIdpConfig.description:
Federated customer-IdP details for a `jwks` or `spa` app (applications-and-oauth.md
§5.0★ / §5.0★★). Provider-agnostic — any OIDC issuer (Okta, Supabase,
Auth0, …); the swap is which issuer/client you register here.

How the federation verifies its subject_token is a property of the
APPLICATION TYPE, not this payload: a `jwks` app's federation needs only
the issuer — the partner IdP's JWT is verified against its JWKS, nothing
is provisioned, and `client_id` / `client_secret` are not used. An `spa`
app's federation brokers through the per-app customers Zitadel org and
requires the partner IdP's `client_id` + `client_secret` (enforced in the
route, which knows the application).

The `client_secret` is write-only — it is encrypted at rest on the
`application_idps` row and forwarded to Zitadel once; it is never returned by
the API.

Required for every provider except 'zitadel' (Introspection-hosted built-in login, which has no external issuer).

CallerContext.description:
Caller environment payload — segment.io-style observability data.

Carried alongside `identity` on `RunRequest` and persisted on the
session so it survives across all tasks the runner spawns. Forwarded
to the sandbox via `TASK_CALLER_CONTEXT` env so the harness can
stamp OTel resource attributes. Echoed back on
`RunnerSpec.runtime_context.caller` for SDK display.

Distinct from `identity` — this payload is **not** consulted by
routing. `hash_key_fields` only walks `identity.user_id` /
`anonymous_id` / `conversation_id`. Mixing routing inputs into the
observability payload would be a privacy + stability footgun
(e.g. routing on IP).

Well-known fields are typed below; any additional fields the caller
passes (segment.io conventions: `app`, `device`, `os`, `campaign`,
`network`, `screen`, `timezone`, `traits`, or custom keys) ride
along verbatim via `extra="allow"`.

Experiment.description:
Full Experiment row.

Used as the API response shape directly — there is no separate
ExperimentResponse wrapper. `deleted_at` is excluded from wire
serialization by the SoftDeleteMixin so the wire payload only
carries fields callers care about. `org_id` is included so
multi-tenant clients (e.g. agents-worker tests) can spot mismatches
without a second lookup.

description.description: Optional operator notes — the experiment's hypothesis / why it's running.
description.title: Description

environment.description: Environment lane this experiment routes inside.

goal_json.description: Composite Goal — see docs/design/runtime-platform.md for the shape. Components reference typed telemetry fields or pinned judge definitions. REQUIRED — it is the reward the bandit optimizes; an adaptive scorer with no goal can't learn and stays uniform-random. DB column is JSONB NOT NULL — an empty component list is rejected at create.

hash_key_fields.description: Baggage / header walk order for the consistent-hash subject.
hash_key_fields.title: Hash Key Fields

runtime_group_id.description: The Runtime group this experiment is testing. All arms must reference Runtime rows in this group.
runtime_group_id.title: Runtime Group Id

sample_rate.description: Fraction of the runtime group's traffic served to this experiment — its blast radius. Out-of-sample subjects get the group's normal production runtime (never scored); in-sample subjects are Thompson-sampled across all arms. Operator-selectable on production (10%–100%); always 100% on every other lane. Frozen once the experiment is running.
sample_rate.title: Sample Rate

scoring_interval_seconds.description: How often the scorer recomputes posteriors + weights.
scoring_interval_seconds.title: Scoring Interval Seconds

Experiment.title: Experiment

ExperimentArm.description:
One arm of an experiment — a synthetic id + a Runtime row + display label.

`id` is the arm's own identity: several arms can share one `runtime_id` and
differ only by `agent_overrides` (e.g. "main baseline" vs "main + overrides"),
so scoring, posterior, weights, and judgement telemetry key on `id`, never on
`runtime_id`.

agent_overrides.description: Entry-point agent remap for this arm: {called_agent -> target_agent} (recipe `agents/*.yaml` names). A called entrypoint matching a key runs the target instead — e.g. {'agent-codex': 'agent-codex2'} — so one runtime serves several arms differing only by agent. Applies ONLY to the session entrypoint, never to subagent dispatch. Resolved at routing time and authoritative for in-sample traffic. Null/empty = the runtime's own default agent resolution.
agent_overrides.title: Agent Overrides

initial_weight.description: System-seeded starting weight (even split across arms). NOT operator-authored — the bandit reallocates from the posterior once scoring begins. Present on reads for observability; absent from the create body.
initial_weight.title: Initial Weight

ExperimentArm.title: ExperimentArm

ExperimentArmCreate.description:
One arm in the create body — a runtime + display label.

Arms are symmetric: there is no operator-selected baseline. The multi-armed
bandit (`beta_sample`) Thompson-samples across all arms equally and allocates
traffic from the posterior, so operators set neither a per-arm weight nor a
control flag. The incumbent (current production version) is simply whichever
runtime currently serves production for the group — out-of-sample traffic is
routed to it via normal production resolution, independent of the experiment.

agent_overrides.description: Optional entry-point agent remap for this arm: {called_agent -> target_agent} (recipe `agents/*.yaml` names). Applies only to the session entrypoint, never to subagents. Omit for no remap (the runtime's own default agent resolution).

Out-of-sample subjects get the group's normal production runtime (never scored); in-sample subjects are Thompson-sampled across all arms. ExperimentRoutingStrategy.description:
How CP picks an arm per runner request.

BETA_SAMPLE is the only strategy — a multi-armed bandit. It is per-request
Thompson sampling seeded by the identity token (reproducible) on every lane;
arms are symmetric (no special baseline). Production exposure is bounded by
the experiment's `sample_rate` (an out-of-sample subject is served the
group's normal production runtime, unattributed), not by a candidate-traffic
cap. In-sample subjects are pinned to one arm for the experiment's lifetime.
(Retained as a single-value enum so the row + API response keep a typed
strategy; hrw / round_robin were removed.)
ExperimentRoutingStrategy.title: ExperimentRoutingStrategy

ExperimentStatus.description:
Experiment lifecycle.

draft → arms can still be edited; routing is not live.
running → routing live; scorer updating posteriors + weights. At most one
    running experiment per (runtime group + environment); starting another
    while one is running is a 409 — end it first.
ended → operator concluded the experiment after reviewing the result
    (terminal; production changes only when the winning PR is merged).
cancelled → operator aborted before a winner (terminal).
ExperimentStatus.title: ExperimentStatus

ExperimentUpdate.description:
PATCH /v1/experiments/{id} — mutable fields.

Status transitions go through dedicated endpoints (start / end /
cancel); runtime_group_id and arms are immutable once the experiment is
running so live routing decisions can never become inconsistent. While
running, only description stays editable; goal_json and sample_rate are
fixed so accumulated evidence keeps a single measured objective and the
sampled population never shifts mid-experiment.

sample_rate.description: Edit the experiment's traffic share (draft only; frozen once running).
sample_rate.title: Sample Rate

materialised access-token claims so DP can stamp it onto

RunnerSpec.description:
Response from `POST /v1/runtimes/{id}/run` and `/v1/experiments/{id}/run`.

Customer-facing wire shape. The SDK holds exactly one credential:
`session_token`. DP transparently materialises the per-request
access tokens it needs via the existing session_materializer
Restate path — the SDK doesn't see or manage them.

Sandbox-internal fields (access_token, refresh_token, egress
credentials map, sandbox bootstrap, DP-enforced limits, Otari
proxy key) live on `InternalRunnerSpec` and are only returned from
`POST /internal/agent-sessions` on the CP→DP path. Keeping them
off the customer wire means a leaked `RunnerSpec` JSON can't
expose materialised JWTs, upstream API keys, repo installation
IDs, or LLM-spend authorization.

session_token.description: Signed session_locator JWT — the customer's long-lived credential. SDK sends it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on every `runner.tasks.*` / `runner.files.*` call. DP recognises `type=session_locator`, looks up the session in its Redis cache, and materialises a short-lived access token transparently via the existing session_materializer Restate path.
session_token.title: Session Token

remainder (see ``DeploymentEnvironment._build_sandbox_providers``). ``endpoint``

delete.description:
Revoke an API key, immediately or after a grace period.

With no ``expires_in`` (or ``0``) the key is soft-deleted and its DP cache
entry invalidated, so it stops working at once. With ``expires_in`` > 0 the
key is scheduled to expire that many seconds from now and keeps working until
then; lookups exclude it once it expires.

parameters.description: Grace period in seconds before the key stops working. Omit or pass 0 to revoke immediately. When > 0 the key keeps working until then, after which it is treated as expired (the UI offers 1h / 1d / 7d).

Add a federated IdP to an existing application.

Broker mode is derived from the application type: `jwks` apps take
`direct_jwks` rows (nothing to provision — `active` immediately), `spa`
apps take `zitadel` rows (the brokered upstream is provisioned in Zitadel
async; the row starts `pending`). The brokered IdP's
`client_secret` is encrypted at rest and never returned. Fails with 409 if
the application already holds the maximum number of active federations.
High-risk (federates an external IdP), so owner-only and gated behind the
`allow_federated_idps` entitlement (Max/Enterprise or developer).

Create a pending integration for this org.

Find-or-create semantics:
- If a pending integration of this type already exists, returns it (200)
- If an active or disabled integration of this type exists, returns 409
- Otherwise creates a new pending row (201) — the caller then drives the
  service-specific activation flow (e.g. GitHub install → PATCH with
  external_id; Slack/Linear OAuth → PATCH with external_id)

The row is always created with ``status=pending`` and no ``external_id``
regardless of request body — the frontend cannot create active rows.

get.description:
Resolve a ``user_code`` for the authenticated ``/activate`` page.

REQUIRES a logged-in CP member (the browser ``intro_cp_session`` cookie,
via ``scoped_auth``) — consent can only be given by an authenticated human.
Returns the requesting client + the scopes the user is about to grant so
consent is informed.

post.description:
Approve or deny a pending device authorization for the ``/activate`` page.

REQUIRES a logged-in CP member. On approval the record is bound to the
approving member's ``member_id`` + ``org_id`` (taken from the OIDC JWT
only — never from the request body) and to the project the member
selected, so the minted token is project-scoped to the developer's chosen
project (like an API key). ``project`` is required to approve and is
validated against the member's org; it is ignored on deny. The CLI's
polling token request then completes.

RFC 8628 §3.1/§3.2 — device authorization request.

Unauthenticated by design: the user has not logged in yet (that is the
whole point of the CLI device flow). Only the first-party `cli` client
may start it. Returns a high-entropy opaque ``device_code`` (polled at
the token endpoint) and a short human ``user_code`` (typed into the
``/activate`` page).

OAuth 2.0 Token Endpoint.

Supports:
- authorization_code: Exchange auth code for tokens (with PKCE verification)
- refresh_token: Get new access token using refresh token
- urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code: CLI device flow (RFC 8628)
- client_credentials: server-to-server Application auth (intro_app_/intro_sk_)

get.description:
Return current user info from the session cookie.

Browser clients call this to check auth status and get user info
for the UI. Auth is resolved from the intro_cp_session cookie by
validate_auth — no token or session ID needed from the client.

Feature flags are evaluated server-side using trusted JWT context
(email, org_id, plan) so the browser cannot spoof targeting
attributes.

post.description: Create a new Runtime row.

delete.description:
Soft-delete a Runtime and evict its baked images on the data plane.

Mirrors project deletion: the DP eviction runs BEFORE the CP row is
soft-deleted and a DP failure aborts the delete, so a deleted runtime
can never strand ready images in the registry. The DP side is one
bounded write (rows flip to ``evicted``); the registry artifact purge
itself runs in the DP's runtime_baker sweep.

patch.description:
Update presentation / config fields and environment assignment.

The `recipe_id` is immutable — create a new Runtime row to pin a
different Recipe. Setting `environment` points that environment at this
row, moving it off any sibling that currently serves it. Only `staging`
may be set manually; `production` is managed by main-branch deployments.

post.description:
Open a runner pinned to this runtime row.

Returns a `RunnerSpec` the SDK uses to talk directly to the right
DP. CP resolves the recipe pin, mints a short-lived RS256 JWT
carrying `runtime_id` + the caller's `identity`, and returns the
DP endpoint to call. No arm picking — for that use
`POST /v1/experiments/{id}/run`.

See `docs/design/sdk-api.md` "The two CP /run endpoints".
